QUALITY GATES — Errors caught before formal submission

— a dry run using the exact same checks as production

Best Practices: Pre-submission validation is identical to production — no surprises at formal submit

across PATS, pre-submission, and production — consistency eliminates surprises.
— DOL staff manage rules via admin panel; no code deployment for rule changes.
— Filers guided to compliance, not just told they failed.
— Errors caught before they become rejections; proven 33% rejection reduction at NARA.
— Filers get tracking IDs within seconds; status updates at every pipeline stage.
— Every submission reaches DISTRIBUTED or generates a documented, alerted exception.
— Audit trail on every rule execution, every validation decision, every status transition.

Data Quality and Validation — Why Satsyil
D. Annual Rollover and Version Changes
ONE SCHEMA CHANGE → everything below updates automatically, no code deployment needed

annual form rollover. When code IS
Best Practices: No deployments during peak filing (Oct 1-15, Jan 1-7) without DOL approval • Feature flags for gradual rollout
Weekly change advisory board with DOL participation • Every deployment logged with approver and validation results
Zero Code Deployment for Annual Rollover
— All routine form year changes handled through configuration metadata, not application code.
— Government staff preview, approve, and activate changes via admin panel; no contractor dependency.
— Blue/green deployment with instant rollback; form year coexistence for concurrent amendment and new-year filing.
— PATS sandbox, developer portal, and notification cycle ensure every software provider is ready before activation.
— UI fields, API schema, documentation, and PATS test cases all auto-generated from DER metadata changes.
— Any deployment reversed within 5 minutes if health checks fail; rollback drilled in staging before every production event.
— NARA: hundreds of metadata schema updates managed through configuration without application redeployment.
— No deployments during Columbus Day and New Year peak periods without DOL emergency approval.
Annual Rollover and Version Changes — Why Satsyil
PHASED CUTOVER — Legacy system keeps running throughout; no disruption to filers
New system takes filings off-peak hours
Best Practices: No service interruption — filers keep filing during entire migration
5% random sample with manual review • 30-day post-cutover monitoring
DOL approves each rule as valid or legacy defect.
All vendors notified of API changes
maintained indefinitely not just during transition.
Best Practices: Every discovered rule has automated test before go-live • Legacy defect inventory — DOL decides perpetuate or correct
Annual developer conference for hands-on transition guidance • Vendor-specific monitoring during cutover • Post-cutover retrospective
300M+ records migrated at NARA — 15x the estimated EFAST2 migration volume; 33% reduction in ingestion failures; 60% improvement in data quality.
Legacy EFAST2 continues serving filers throughout migration; incremental delta loads eliminate data gaps.
Source-to-target validation at every batch; daily reconciliation reports; DOL-accessible live dashboard.
Pre-defined criteria (>1% reconciliation failure, >5% processing error) remove subjectivity; 5-minute rollback SLA.
AI-assisted code analysis + historical data mining + incumbent KT + shadow mode validation; gap analysis report within 60 days.
90-day advance notice, dedicated sandbox, named support engineers, weekly office hours, 72-hour cutover hotline, 30-day compatibility mode.
Read → Non-peak write → Full cutover with DOL go/no-go gates at every phase.
No proprietary lock-in; all data, code, and configurations government-owned and exportable.
Data Migration and Transition Planning — Why Satsyil

EFAST2 undergoes mandatory annual updates driven by regulatory changes, new DER artifacts, and form revisions. This is one of the highest-risk operational events in the system's calendar.

The core question here is: Every year, DOL updates the Form 5500 — new fields are added, rules change, schedules are modified. How do we handle that without breaking the system, without downtime, and without making third-party software vendors scramble to update?
1. Adding or modifying fields and validations — how we make changes to what the form looks like and what rules apply.
2. Annual rollover and version changes — how we switch from one year's form to the next.
3. Configuration-driven or metadata-driven updates — how we make changes through settings instead of code.
4. Minimizing code changes and UI redeployment — how we avoid having to rebuild and redeploy the software for routine changes.
Our answer to all four items comes down to one architectural decision: the form is not built in code — it is built from a schema.
Satsyil's approach eliminates the traditional deployment-night risk: configuration-driven updates mean the annual rollover is a scheduled metadata activation, not a code release.
Data quality is the single largest driver of rejected EFAST2 submissions, downstream processing exceptions, and contact center call volume.
Satsyil's approach: prevent errors at the point of entry through a multi-gate validation architecture, return actionable error guidance, and give DOL configurable control over every rule.

Think of field-level validation as spell-check for each individual box on the form. As the filer types, we check: Is this the right kind of data? Is it the right length? Is it within the allowed range? For example, an EIN must be exactly 9 digits with a valid IRS prefix. A plan number must be exactly 3 digits.

Cross-field validation is like checking that the whole story makes sense. For example: Do the total assets on Schedule H match what is reported on Schedule A? Is the number of terminated participants less than the total at the beginning of the year? Does net income
revenues minus expenses? If a plan's participant count jumped from 50 to 5,000 in one year, that gets flagged as suspicious.

We also compare this year's filing against last year's filing. If something changed dramatically — like total assets dropping 90% — we flag it so the filer can confirm it is correct before submitting.

The rules that determine whether a filing is acceptable are stored as configuration data — like entries in a settings table — not as code written into the application. This is important because it means DOL staff can add, change, or retire a rule through an admin panel without needing a software developer to write and deploy new code.

Each rule is tagged with a form year, so we always apply the right rules to the right filing. A 2025 filing gets 2025 rules, and a 2026 filing gets 2026 rules, even if both are being processed at the same time.
DOL controls the severity of each rule: a 'fatal' rule means the filing cannot be submitted until the error is fixed; a 'warning' means the filing can go
but the filer is alerted; 'informational' is just a helpful note. DOL can change a rule's severity without any code changes.
Every time a rule fires — pass or fail — we log it with a timestamp and the filing ID, so there is a complete audit trail DOL can review at any time.

When something is wrong, we show the filer a plain-language message right next to the field that has the problem. Not a cryptic error code like 'E-4072' — instead, something like 'The Sponsor EIN must be in the format NN-NNNNNNN with a valid IRS prefix.'

The filer can fix the error right where it is — they do not have to start the form over. Their previous valid entries are preserved. The form auto-saves their progress, so if they need to step away, they can come back and pick up where they left off. This eliminates the data-loss problem that exists in the current system where a 15-minute timeout erases unsaved work.

For third-party software providers who submit filings in bulk via API, we return structured error responses in a format their software can process automatically — they do not have to manually interpret error messages.
Our contact center agents see the exact same error details as the filer, so when someone calls for help, the agent already knows what the problem is.

We use the same validation engine in the testing sandbox (PATS) and in production, so third-party developers never get surprised by different behavior when they go live. We provide monthly reports to DOL showing the most common errors, error rates by form type, and how often filers successfully correct errors on the first attempt. Every rule is traceable back to its regulatory source in the DER.
PRE-SUBMISSION VALIDATION TO REDUCE REJECTED SUBMISSIONS:

The single most effective way to reduce rejected submissions is to let filers find and fix errors before they formally submit. We provide a 'dry run' capability — the filer can run the complete validation check without
a formal filing. It uses the exact same checks as the real submission process, so there are no surprises.
We also pre-fill the form with last year's accepted data, so the filer only needs to update what changed. This dramatically reduces manual data entry errors.
For third-party software vendors like Relius,

, we provide a pre-validation API. Their software can check a filing against all our rules before the filer even clicks submit. This means errors get caught in the vendor's software, not after the filing reaches EFAST2.
We also provide a PATS sandbox — a testing environment that is identical to production — where vendors can certify their software works correctly before going live.
This is not theoretical. At NARA, we implemented the same kind of pre-validation gates and reduced ingestion failures by 33%. We expect similar or better results for EFAST2.

The five quality gates shown on the slide are the checkpoints every filing must pass before acceptance: (1) Is the data structure valid? (2) Do all business rules pass? (3) Is the employer/plan entity recognized? (4) Are all required attachments present and virus-free? (5) Is this a duplicate of something already submitted?

The moment we receive a filing, we issue a unique tracking ID — within seconds, before any processing begins. The filer gets confirmation three ways at once: on their screen, by email, and on their filing dashboard.

From that point on, the filer can check the status of their filing at any time. The status moves through a clear lifecycle: Received, Validating, Accepted, Processing, and Distributed. Each step is visible on the dashboard.
If something goes wrong during validation, we do not just send a generic rejection. We send back specific details about what failed and what the filer needs to do to fix it — in plain language.

For third-party software vendors who submit many filings at once, we offer webhook notifications. This means their software automatically receives status updates without having to repeatedly check our system.
We keep all of these status records for seven years, so DOL can audit the complete lifecycle of any filing at any time.

The pre-submission validation uses the exact same engine as formal submission — if it passes pre-validation, it will pass formal submission. No surprises. When we reject a filing, we list every single failing check — not just the first one we found. This lets filers fix everything in one pass instead of going back and forth multiple times.
We are confident this approach will significantly reduce rejected EFAST2 submissions, improve the filer experience, and give DOL full visibility into every validation decision.
Satsyil doesn't just build validation — we build
that reduce burden on DOL staff.

The architecture diagram at the top shows how our system works. At the center is a database of JSON Schemas — think of these as the 'blueprints' for each year's form. When DOL needs to make a change, an authorized DOL staff member edits the schema through an admin panel. No software developer needs to be involved.

When that schema is saved, everything downstream updates automatically: the filing form that filers see in their browser, the API that third-party software uses to submit filings, the validation rules, the API documentation, and the testing sandbox. One change, everything updates. No code deployment needed.
ADDING OR MODIFYING FIELDS AND VALIDATIONS:

In practical terms, if DOL needs to add a new field to the Form 5500 for next year, a DOL staff member opens the admin panel, defines the field — its name, what type of data it holds, what format it should be in, what validation rules apply, and what date it should become active — and saves it. That is it. The filing form will show the new field. The API will accept the new data. The validation rules will enforce it. The testing sandbox will include it. All automatically.

Before activating any change, the admin panel shows DOL how many previously-submitted filings would have been affected by the new rule. This lets DOL calibrate whether a rule should be fatal, warning, or informational before turning it on.

On January 1, the new form year activates automatically at midnight — zero downtime. We use a technique called blue/green deployment: the new version runs alongside the old one, and we switch traffic over. If anything goes wrong, we switch back in under 5 minutes.

Prior-year forms stay active so filers can still file amendments for previous years. A filer amending a 2025 filing gets 2025 rules; a filer submitting a new 2026 filing gets 2026 rules. Both work at the same time.

Third-party software vendors get 90 days advance notice of all changes plus access to a testing sandbox where they can certify their software works correctly with the new form year before it goes live.

We run an impact analysis before activating any new rule — DOL can see exactly how many filings would have been affected. We hold an annual developer conference to walk vendors through all upcoming changes. And we give vendors a 90-day deprecation window before retiring any old API version, so no one is caught off guard.
The timeline at the top shows how a typical annual rollover works — and notice that at no point does anyone need to deploy new code.
In July, DOL finalizes the form changes for the next year. In August, a DOL staff member updates the schema in the admin panel. In September, the testing sandbox opens for

so they have 90 days to certify their software. In October, we run a peak-season load test to make sure the system can handle Columbus Day filing volume with the new schemas. And on January 1, the new form year activates automatically at midnight with zero downtime.

The key idea is that the form's structure — what fields exist, what type of data they hold, what rules apply — is all stored as configuration data, not as application code. Think of it like the difference between a word processor and a printed book. The word processor (our application) stays the same; the document you are editing (the schema) changes. You do not need to rebuild the word processor to write a new document.

DOL staff manage these changes directly through the admin panel. They do not need to submit a request and wait for a contractor's development cycle. Every change is version-controlled — meaning we can see what was changed, who changed it, when, and why. If something goes wrong, we can roll back to the previous version in minutes.

This is not a new idea for us. At NARA, we have managed hundreds of schema updates this exact way — through configuration changes — without ever redeploying the application. It is our proven operating model.
MINIMIZING CODE CHANGES AND UI REDEPLOYMENT NEEDS:

Because the filing form renders dynamically from the schema, adding a new field to the form requires exactly zero lines of UI code to be written. The form reads the schema at startup and builds itself. Change the schema, and the form changes.

Validation rules also read from configuration at runtime — no application restart needed. When a new rule is scheduled to activate on a future date, it simply becomes active on that date. No deployment required.

API documentation is auto-generated from the schema, so it is always in sync with what the system actually does. Developer portal documentation, testing sandbox test cases, and schema files all update automatically from the same source.

Our target is zero code deployments for routine annual form rollover. All changes handled through the configuration pipeline. When code changes ARE needed for enhancements or bug fixes, we can deploy in under 30 minutes with zero downtime and roll back in 5 minutes.

We do not deploy anything during peak filing periods (October 1-15 and January 1-7) without explicit DOL approval. We use feature flags to gradually roll out new features — test with a small group before everyone sees it. We hold a weekly change advisory board meeting with DOL participation before scheduling any production changes. And every deployment is logged with who approved it and the results of post-deployment validation.

At NARA, we have managed hundreds of schema updates through configuration without ever redeploying the application. That is our target for EFAST2 as well — zero code deployments for routine annual form rollover.

The key message: annual rollover goes from being a high-risk software deployment event to a routine administrative task that DOL controls directly. Satsyil's architecture reduces it from a high-risk code deployment to a low-risk configuration activation.

The combination of configuration-driven updates, blue/green deployment, 90-day third-party readiness, and deployment blackout windows reflects operational maturity derived from running production federal filing systems.

Data migration is the highest-risk event in the entire EFAST2 modernization program. Satsyil brings direct, demonstrated experience migrating federal data. We migrated over 300 million records at NARA — 15 times the size of EFAST2's filing history. We know how to do this at scale.
1. Large-scale data and document migration — how we move 20+ years of filing data and millions of documents to the new system.
2. Validation and reconciliation — how we make sure every record that was in the old system is correctly in the new system.
3. Rollback planning — what happens if something goes wrong, and how we reverse course quickly.
4. Handling undocumented logic and embedded business rules — how we find and preserve rules that exist in the old system's code but were never formally documented.
5. Transition support for third-party vendors — how we help the companies whose software submits filings to EFAST2 transition to the new system.

The diagram at the top shows our phased approach. We do not do a 'big bang' switch. Instead, we move traffic to the new system in three phases, with DOL making a formal go/no-go decision between each phase.

Phase 1: The new system handles only read queries — status lookups, filing history. The old system still handles all new filings. This lets us validate the new system under real traffic without any risk.
Phase 2: The new system starts accepting new filings, but only during off-peak hours. The old system is still primary during peak periods. We compare results from both systems.
Phase 3: All traffic moves to the new system. The old system stays available in read-only mode for 90 days as a safety net.
ITEM ① — LARGE-SCALE DATA AND DOCUMENT MIGRATION:

We use automated pipelines to migrate everything — filing records, plan data, filer profiles, and all the PDF and XML attachments. We migrate the most recent years first because those are the ones filers query most often. Historical data follows in later phases.
During the migration, the old system keeps running. New filings submitted to the old system during migration are captured by incremental loads, so there is no data gap when we switch over.
Every document is verified using SHA-256 hashing — essentially a digital fingerprint. If the fingerprint of the migrated document does not match the original, we flag it and reprocess it.
This is not theoretical. We migrated over 300 million records at NARA using this exact approach.
Reconciliation means proving that every record in the old system is correctly in the new system. We do this continuously, not as a one-time check.

At every batch of migration, we compare record counts between the old and new systems. We re-validate every field against the current rules. We verify document hashes. We do statistical sampling — checking 5% of records at random with full manual review of anything that looks wrong.
DOL gets daily reconciliation reports and has access to a live dashboard showing migration progress. Before we switch any production traffic, DOL must formally sign off on the reconciliation results.
After cutover, we continue reconciliation monitoring daily for 30 more days.
If something goes wrong, we can switch traffic back to the old system in under 5 minutes. The old system stays running in read-only mode for 90 days after cutover specifically for this purpose.

We have pre-defined automatic triggers: if the reconciliation failure rate exceeds 1%, or the processing error rate exceeds 5%, rollback fires automatically. DOL does not have to call anyone — it just happens.

We also rehearse the rollback procedure in a staging environment 30 days before the actual go-live, and DOL observes the drill. If rollback is ever triggered for real, we deliver a root cause analysis within 24 hours.
HANDLING UNDOCUMENTED LOGIC AND EMBEDDED BUSINESS RULES:

This is often the most dangerous part of replacing a legacy system. Over the years, rules get added to the code that were never formally documented. The people who wrote them may have moved on. Nobody remembers why they exist. But they affect whether filings get accepted or rejected. We address this with a four-step discovery process:

Step 1 — AI Code Analysis: We use machine learning tools to scan the legacy codebase and automatically identify conditional logic, edge-case handlers, and embedded rules that are not documented anywhere.

Step 2 — Historical Data Mining: We analyze 10+ years of filing outcomes to reverse-engineer rules. If certain filings were consistently rejected for reasons not explained by the documented rules, those hidden rules become visible through the patterns in the data.

Step 3 — Incumbent Knowledge Transfer: We conduct a 30-day structured knowledge transfer with the current contractor, including workshops, code walkthroughs, and systematic interviews with their operations and development staff to capture tribal knowledge.

Step 4 — Rule Registry and DOL Approval: We document every discovered rule in a formal registry with its source, its regulatory basis (if any), and automated test cases. Then DOL makes the decision for each one: Is this a legitimate rule that should be preserved? Or is it a legacy bug that should be corrected? We do not perpetuate bugs disguised as features.
We deliver a gap analysis report to DOL within 60 days of contract award listing every undocumented rule we found and our proposed treatment.
After cutover, we continue monitoring for 90 days, comparing outcomes against the legacy baseline to catch any rules we may have missed.
TRANSITION SUPPORT FOR EXTERNAL INTEGRATORS AND THIRD-PARTY VENDORS:
EFAST2 depends on a large ecosystem of third-party software companies — Relius,
, and many others — whose software submits filings on behalf of plan administrators. If their transition fails, filers cannot file. Our transition plan has four phases:
First, 90-day advance notice. All registered software vendors are notified of every API change, schema update, and the cutover schedule well before anything happens.
Second, we open a testing sandbox — a copy of the production system — where vendors can test their software against the new system. This is available 90 days before cutover.

Third, a compatibility bridge. We accept both the old format and the new format at the same time during the transition period. Vendors can migrate on their own timeline — no one is forced into a sudden switch.

Fourth, cutover support. We staff a 24/7 technical hotline for 72 hours around the cutover. Major vendors get a named engineer as their point of contact. Vendors who have not finished transitioning get a 30-day grace period where we continue accepting filings in the old format.
After the transition, we maintain a permanent developer support channel — not just during the cutover window, but permanently.

Every rule we discover gets an automated test case before the new system goes live — no untested rules in production. DOL decides whether to keep or fix each legacy rule. We hold an annual developer conference for vendors. We monitor each vendor's submission volumes and error rates individually during cutover. And we conduct a post-cutover retrospective with major vendors to capture lessons learned.
Satsyil has proven to completed federal data migration at a scale of 300M+ records with documented quality improvement outcomes.
The combination of phased cutover, quantitative rollback triggers, and 90-day third-party transition support reflects a migration plan designed for zero-risk execution, not just zero-risk planning
